Name: Andaman Woodpecker
Best_seen_at: Mount Harriet National Park, South Andaman
How_to_identify: A large, all-black woodpecker endemic to the Andaman Islands. Male has a full red crest and malar stripe; female has red only on the hindcrest and lacks the red malar. Distinct from the White-bellied Woodpecker by its entirely dark underparts.
Diet: Ants, termites, and wood-boring beetle larvae

Name: Greater Yellownape
Habitat: Subtropical and tropical moist lowland and montane forests
Best_seen_at: Jim Corbett National Park (Uttarakhand), Kaziranga National Park (Assam)
How_to_identify: Large green woodpecker with a prominent bushy yellow crest/nape. Throat is yellow (male) or rufous-brown (female). Distinguished from Lesser Yellownape by larger size and lack of red on the crown.

Name: Lesser Yellownape
Habitat: Open forests, deciduous and evergreen woodlands
How_to_identify: Medium-sized green woodpecker with a yellow nape and red markings on the crown. Male has a red malar stripe; female does not. Smaller than Greater Yellownape and shows more red on the head.

Name: Streak-throated Woodpecker
Habitat: Open deciduous forests, secondary growth, and groves
Best_seen_at: Keoladeo National Park (Rajasthan), Bandhavgarh National Park (Madhya Pradesh)
How_to_identify: Green upperparts with a yellow rump. Underparts are pale with heavy dark scales and streaks. Throat is distinctly streaked with grey/black. Male has a red crown; female has a blackish crown.
Diet: Mainly ants and termites, often forages on the ground

Name: Scaly-bellied Woodpecker
Best_seen_at: Pangot (Uttarakhand), Great Himalayan National Park (Himachal Pradesh)
How_to_identify: Large green woodpecker with a diagnostic 'scaly' pattern on the belly. Yellow-green rump and black-and-white barred tail. Male has a red crown; female has a black crown.
Diet: Ants, termites, and occasionally berries or nectar

Name: Grey-headed Woodpecker
Best_seen_at: Jim Corbett National Park (Uttarakhand), Mishmi Hills
How_to_identify: Uniform grey head and neck with a thin black malar stripe. Body is green. Male has a small red patch on the forehead; female has a plain grey forehead. Lacks the scaly belly of P. squamatus.
Diet: Ants, termites, and other insects; frequently forages on the ground

Name: Himalayan Flameback
Habitat: Sub-Himalayan sal forests and moist deciduous forests
Best_seen_at: Jim Corbett National Park (Uttarakhand), Dudhwa National Park (Uttar Pradesh)
How_to_identify: Similar to Common Flameback but larger. Features a brownish/buff throat with a central dark stripe (not solid black). Male has a red crown; female has a black forehead with white streaks and red hind-crest.

Name: Common Flameback
Habitat: Mangroves, coastal forests, and open woodlands
How_to_identify: Three-toed woodpecker with a golden-yellow back. Distinguished from Black-rumped Flameback by having only one black malar stripe (not two) and a red rump. Smallest of the flamebacks.

Name: Black-rumped Flameback
Best_seen_at: Common across most of India (e.g., Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata)
How_to_identify: Diagnostic black rump and white-spotted black throat. Golden-yellow wings and red crown (male). Female has a black forehead with white spots. Very vocal and common in urban settings.

Name: Greater Flameback
Best_seen_at: Kaziranga National Park (Assam), Thattekad Bird Sanctuary (Kerala)
How_to_identify: Largest of the flamebacks with a prominent ivory-white bill. Features a split black malar stripe and a white hind-neck. Male has a full red crest; female has a black crest with white spots.
Diet: Insects and larvae, particularly from large tree trunks
